12th-army-group.name: 12th Army Group
12th-army-group.history: Primary American army group in the European Theater, headquartered in Luxembourg City.

first-army.name: First United States Army
first-army.history: Bore the brunt of the initial German assault in the Ardennes. Temporarily placed under British command during the battle.

third-army.name: Third United States Army
third-army.commander: Lt. Gen. George S. Patton Jr.
third-army.history: Executed a remarkable 90-degree turn to relieve Bastogne, moving three divisions over 100 miles in 48 hours during winter conditions.

v-corps.name: V Corps
v-corps.history: Held the northern shoulder of the Bulge, preventing German expansion northward.

viii-corps.name: VIII Corps
viii-corps.history: Thinly stretched across the Ardennes, bore the full weight of the initial German attack along the Schnee Eifel.

xviii-airborne-corps.name: XVIII Airborne Corps
xviii-airborne-corps.history: Rushed to the Ardennes from strategic reserve in France. Deployed to plug gaps in the American lines.

iii-corps.name: III Corps
iii-corps.history: Spearheaded Patton's counterattack to relieve Bastogne from the south.

xii-corps.name: XII Corps
xii-corps.history: Protected the southern flank of the Bulge and supported the counteroffensive.

2nd-infantry.name: 2nd Infantry Division
2nd-infantry.history: Held Elsenborn Ridge, a critical defensive position on the northern shoulder of the Bulge.

99th-infantry.name: 99th Infantry Division
99th-infantry.history: A green division that fought tenaciously at the Battle of Elsenborn Ridge despite being heavily outnumbered.

106th-infantry.name: 106th Infantry Division
106th-infantry.history: Arrived at the front only 5 days before the offensive. Two of its three regiments were encircled and forced to surrender on the Schnee Eifel — one of the largest mass surrenders in US history.

28th-infantry.name: 28th Infantry Division
28th-infantry.history: Defending along the Our River, fought a desperate delaying action that significantly slowed the German advance toward Bastogne.

4th-infantry.name: 4th Infantry Division
4th-infantry.history: Defended the southern shoulder of the Bulge in the Echternach-Luxembourg area.

82nd-airborne.name: 82nd Airborne Division
82nd-airborne.history: Deployed to the Werbomont-Stoumont area to block Kampfgruppe Peiper's advance. Fought along the Salm River.

101st-airborne.name: 101st Airborne Division
101st-airborne.history: Rushed to Bastogne by truck convoy on Dec 19. Held the vital crossroads town under complete encirclement. McAuliffe's famous 'NUTS!' reply to German surrender demand became legendary.

7th-armored.name: 7th Armored Division
7th-armored.history: Rushed to St. Vith and organized a horseshoe-shaped defensive perimeter that held for 7 critical days (Dec 17-23).

9th-armored.name: 9th Armored Division
9th-armored.history: Elements fought at both St. Vith and Bastogne. Combat Command B fought alongside the 7th Armored at St. Vith, while Combat Command R defended approaches to Bastogne.

4th-armored.name: 4th Armored Division
4th-armored.history: Spearhead of Patton's relief force. Broke through to Bastogne on December 26 after fierce fighting.

26th-infantry.name: 26th Infantry Division
26th-infantry.history: Part of Patton's relief force attacking northward toward Bastogne.

80th-infantry.name: 80th Infantry Division
80th-infantry.history: Advanced alongside the 4th Armored Division during the Bastogne relief operation.

5th-infantry.name: 5th Infantry Division
5th-infantry.history: Protected the southern shoulder of the Bulge near the Sauer River.

army-group-b.name: Army Group B
army-group-b.history: Commanded all German forces in the Ardennes offensive (Operation Watch on the Rhine).

6th-panzer-army.name: 6th Panzer Army
6th-panzer-army.history: Main effort of the offensive, attacking in the north toward Liège. Composed primarily of SS divisions.

5th-panzer-army.name: 5th Panzer Army
5th-panzer-army.history: Achieved the deepest penetration of the offensive, nearly reaching the Meuse River. Encircled Bastogne.

7th-army.name: 7th Army
7th-army.history: Protected the southern flank of the offensive. Composed mostly of infantry without significant armor.

i-ss-panzer-corps.name: I SS Panzer Corps
i-ss-panzer-corps.history: Spearhead of the 6th Panzer Army. Included the infamous Kampfgruppe Peiper.

lxvii-corps.name: LXVII Corps
lxvii-corps.history: Infantry corps supporting the northern attack, tasked with breaking through the Elsenborn Ridge area.

xlvii-panzer-corps.name: XLVII Panzer Corps
xlvii-panzer-corps.history: Encircled Bastogne and pushed furthest west toward the Meuse. Demanded Bastogne's surrender.

lxvi-corps.name: LXVI Corps
lxvi-corps.history: Attacked St. Vith and encircled two regiments of the 106th Infantry Division.

lviii-panzer-corps.name: LVIII Panzer Corps
lviii-panzer-corps.history: Attacked through the center of the Ardennes, aiming for crossings over the Ourthe River.

lxxx-corps.name: LXXX Corps
lxxx-corps.history: Southern-most corps in the offensive, protecting the left flank.

1st-ss-panzer.name: 1st SS Panzer Division Leibstandarte
1st-ss-panzer.history: Elite SS division. Kampfgruppe Peiper, its spearhead battle group, committed the Malmedy massacre and advanced furthest before being cut off.

12th-ss-panzer.name: 12th SS Panzer Division Hitlerjugend
12th-ss-panzer.history: Young but experienced SS division that fought fiercely around the twin villages of Krinkelt-Rocherath.

12th-vgd.name: 12th Volksgrenadier Division
12th-vgd.history: Infantry division that opened the gap for the 12th SS Panzer Division in the Losheim Gap area.

3rd-para.name: 3rd Parachute Division
3rd-para.history: Elite parachute infantry supporting the northern attack. Despite their name, they fought as regular infantry.

277th-vgd.name: 277th Volksgrenadier Division
277th-vgd.history: Attacked the 99th Infantry Division positions near the Elsenborn Ridge.

326th-vgd.name: 326th Volksgrenadier Division
326th-vgd.history: Supported the attack on Elsenborn Ridge alongside the 277th VGD.

2nd-panzer.name: 2nd Panzer Division
2nd-panzer.history: Reached the furthest western point of the offensive near Celles, just 4 miles from the Meuse River, before being destroyed by Allied forces.

panzer-lehr.name: Panzer Lehr Division
panzer-lehr.history: Veteran demonstration division that attempted to bypass Bastogne to the south.

26th-vgd.name: 26th Volksgrenadier Division
26th-vgd.history: Formed the infantry cordon around Bastogne during the siege.

18th-vgd.name: 18th Volksgrenadier Division
18th-vgd.history: Encircled and captured two regiments of the US 106th Infantry Division on the Schnee Eifel.

62nd-vgd.name: 62nd Volksgrenadier Division
62nd-vgd.history: Supported the encirclement at St. Vith alongside the 18th VGD.

116th-panzer.name: 116th Panzer Division
116th-panzer.history: Known as the 'Greyhound Division'. Attempted to reach the Meuse via Hotton and Marche.

560th-vgd.name: 560th Volksgrenadier Division
560th-vgd.history: Infantry support for the 116th Panzer Division in the central sector.

352nd-vgd.name: 352nd Volksgrenadier Division
352nd-vgd.history: Protected the southern flank of the offensive. Successor to the 352nd Division that defended Omaha Beach.

ii-ss-panzer-corps.name: II SS Panzer Corps
ii-ss-panzer-corps.history: Held in reserve initially, committed to the battle around Dec 21 to reinforce the stalled 6th Panzer Army offensive near Elsenborn Ridge.

lxxxv-corps.name: LXXXV Corps
lxxxv-corps.history: Southern corps of the 7th Army, responsible for protecting the deepest southern flank of the offensive.

2nd-ss-panzer.name: 2nd SS Panzer Division Das Reich
2nd-ss-panzer.history: Veteran SS panzer division notorious for the Oradour-sur-Glane massacre in France. Committed as reserve to exploit any breakthrough in the northern sector.

9th-ss-panzer.name: 9th SS Panzer Division Hohenstaufen
9th-ss-panzer.history: Fought at Arnhem in September 1944. Committed to the Ardennes as part of II SS Panzer Corps reserve, saw action near Manhay and Grandmenil.

9th-panzer.name: 9th Panzer Division
9th-panzer.history: Committed as a reserve division to reinforce the drive toward the Meuse. Fought in the Bastogne corridor area.

3rd-panzergrenadier.name: 3rd Panzergrenadier Division
3rd-panzergrenadier.history: Committed to reinforce the Bastogne siege, attacking from the southeast. Fought in the corridor battles around Bastogne in late December.

15th-panzergrenadier.name: 15th Panzergrenadier Division
15th-panzergrenadier.history: Veteran division from the Italian campaign. Committed to reinforce operations around Bastogne in late December.

276th-vgd.name: 276th Volksgrenadier Division
276th-vgd.history: Attacked on the southern flank alongside the 352nd VGD, protecting the left wing of the offensive against the US 4th Infantry Division.

5th-para.name: 5th Parachute Division
5th-para.history: Fought on the southern flank, crossing the Sauer River to attack American positions. Tasked with blocking Patton's Third Army relief force approaching from the south.

fuhrer-begleit.name: Führer Begleit Brigade
fuhrer-begleit.history: Elite brigade commanded by Otto Remer, who helped suppress the July 20 plot. Committed at St. Vith on Dec 18, helped break the American defensive perimeter.

fuhrer-grenadier.name: Führer Grenadier Brigade
fuhrer-grenadier.history: Motorized brigade committed as reserve to reinforce the Bastogne sector. Fought in the corridor battles east of Bastogne.

150th-panzer-brigade.name: 150th Panzer Brigade
150th-panzer-brigade.history: Commanded by Otto Skorzeny for Operation Greif. English-speaking commandos in captured Allied uniforms spread confusion behind lines. The conventional brigade element failed to capture the Meuse bridges. Withdrawn by end of December.

stosser-group.name: Operation Stösser Paratroopers
stosser-group.history: Approximately 1,300 paratroopers dropped on Dec 17 near Baraque Michel to seize the Eupen-Malmedy road junction. Badly scattered by high winds and inexperienced pilots, only about 300 assembled. Surrendered on Dec 22 after failing to accomplish their mission.

vii-corps.name: VII Corps
vii-corps.history: Known as 'Lightning Joe' Collins' corps. Deployed to the western shoulder to stop the German advance toward the Meuse.

2nd-armored.name: 2nd Armored Division
2nd-armored.history: Destroyed the 2nd Panzer Division at Celles on Christmas Day, stopping the deepest German penetration just 4 miles from the Meuse River.

3rd-armored.name: 3rd Armored Division
3rd-armored.history: Fought in the northern shoulder area, engaging German forces near Hotton and helping seal the western end of the Bulge.

6th-armored.name: 6th Armored Division
6th-armored.history: Committed south of Bastogne in late December, fought to expand the corridor to the besieged town.

10th-armored.name: 10th Armored Division
10th-armored.history: CCB under Col. William Roberts was among the first reinforcements to reach Bastogne on Dec 18. Teams Cherry, Desobry, and O'Hara fought delaying actions on eastern approaches.

11th-armored.name: 11th Armored Division
11th-armored.history: Green division arriving Dec 29, committed to fighting west of Bastogne in early January. Took heavy casualties learning combat the hard way.

1st-infantry.name: 1st Infantry Division
1st-infantry.history: Committed to Elsenborn Ridge on Dec 17, reinforcing the 2nd and 99th Divisions. Helped form the unbreakable northern shoulder of the Bulge.

30th-infantry.name: 30th Infantry Division
30th-infantry.history: Blocked Kampfgruppe Peiper at Stoumont and Stavelot, trapping his force in the La Gleize pocket. The division's determined defense was crucial in halting the 6th Panzer Army's advance.

75th-infantry.name: 75th Infantry Division
75th-infantry.history: A green division committed in late December to help contain the western face of the Bulge near Hotton and Grandmenil.

83rd-infantry.name: 83rd Infantry Division
83rd-infantry.history: Committed to the western shoulder in late December, fought in the counteroffensive pushing east through January.

84th-infantry.name: 84th Infantry Division
84th-infantry.history: Fought at Marche-en-Famenne, helping stop the 116th Panzer Division and 2nd Panzer Division from reaching the Meuse.

87th-infantry.name: 87th Infantry Division
87th-infantry.history: Part of Patton's Third Army, committed to fighting southwest of Bastogne in late December.

17th-airborne.name: 17th Airborne Division
17th-airborne.history: Arrived from England on Christmas Day. Committed to action west of Bastogne in early January, the division's first combat.

705th-td.name: 705th Tank Destroyer Battalion
705th-td.history: Attached to 101st Airborne at Bastogne, provided critical anti-tank defense with M18 Hellcats during the siege.
